Title: MRT cable theft persists despite tighter security, RM1.28mil lost [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:  Cable theft syndicates continue to target Mass Rapid Transit (MRT) infrastructure in the Klang Valley despite heightened scrutiny with 16 cases recorded since January resulting in losses of RM1.28 million.
Prasarana Group chief health, safety, security, environment and sustainable development officer Idzqandar Abu Bakar said the thefts persisted even as patrols, K-9 units and closed-circuit television (CCTV) surveillance were intensified along vulnerable stretches.
"From January to now, we have recorded 16 cases. Each case results in losses of  RM60,000 to RM80,000 depending on the size and extent of the cables stolen.
"That figure is based on the actual cable cost, not the replacement cost, which is  higher."
He said this during a site inspection at a  hotspot along the MRT alignment in Sungai Besi on Friday.
He said the real impact goes beyond the value of stolen cables, as entire sections must be replaced following each incident.
"We can't just splice in a cable to reconnect the segment that was stolen. The entire segment, sometimes spanning at least 100m  from one connection point to another, has to be replaced.
"This is to avoid signal loss and further disruptions to train operations."
Idzqandar said the syndicates  operate at night when tracks are de-energised, allowing them a window of 10 to 15 minutes to cut and remove cables.
[[nid:1415612]]
He said thieves have adopted increasingly sophisticated methods, including cutting cables in concrete trunking systems before dropping  them  for accomplices to collect later.
The thefts primarily involve power cables, although signal, communication and off-current cables have also been targeted, with the latter posing serious risks to train operations.
"Even if some cables do not have high copper value, cutting communication cables can  affect our ability to detect train positions, which is critical for safe operations."
Prasarana has identified 13 hotspot locations along MRT alignments, with the top five being Phileo Damansara, Serdang Depot, Taman Naga Emas, Kuchai and Cyberjaya.
To curb the problem, the company has ramped up patrols, including deploying  dogs capable of detecting intrusions and hidden tools used in theft activities.
"Using dogs allows us to cover larger areas more efficiently compared with relying solely on manpower. They can quickly detect suspicious movement and even locate hidden equipment used for cable theft."
Short-term measures  include reinforcing security barriers, improving lighting in vulnerable areas and installing CCTV systems where feasible.
For longer-term mitigation, Prasarana plans to install  protective covers over cable infrastructure and boost detection capabilities using technology.
Despite these measures, Idzqandar acknowledged that enforcement remains challenging given the scale of MRT alignments, with some hotspot areas stretching up to 200m.
Earlier this year, it was reported that  syndicates had infiltrated MRT infrastructure using viaduct access points, billboards and even concealed "living spaces" beneath elevated tracks to facilitate theft activities.
The issue has cost Prasarana at least RM21 million over the past three years and  disrupted rail operations, with cable theft cases surging year-on-year from 42 incidents in 2024 to 72 last year.
[[nid:1415611]]
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Netanyahu slams Erdogan over comments on Iran ceasefire
Article:
JERUSALEM: Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu sharply criticised Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan on Saturday after comments he made about the US-Iran ceasefire, vowing that Israel would continue fighting Teheran and its regional proxies.
Shortly after the United States and Iran agreed to a ceasefire earlier this week, Erdogan warned US President Donald Trump of "possible provocations and sabotage" that could undermine the agreement, without specifying who might threaten the deal.
On Saturday, Netanyahu responded by criticising the Turkish leader.
"Israel, under my leadership, will continue to fight Iran's terror regime and its proxies, unlike Erdogan who accommodates them and massacred his own Kurdish citizens," Netanyahu said on X.
During his phone call with Trump, Erdogan also urged that the truce "not be compromised under any circumstances," saying Turkiye would offer its full support to ensure as much.
Turkiye, while a fierce critic of Israel, joined diplomatic efforts with Egypt and Pakistan to reach a ceasefire in the conflict.
Later on Saturday, Israeli Defence Minister Israel Katz also lashed out at Erdogan on X, calling him a "paper tiger."
Erdogan "who did not respond to missile fire from Iran into Turkish territory and has proven to be a paper tiger, is now fleeing into the realm of antisemitism and calling for show trials in Turkiye against Israel's political and military leadership."
"What an absurdity. A man of the Muslim Brotherhood, who massacred the Kurds, accuses Israel – defending itself against his Hamas allies – of genocide."
"Israel will continue to defend itself with strength and determination – and he would do well to remain silent."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Long-haul express bus trips may see delays, says association
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Long-haul express bus trips may face delays due to diesel purchase caps and fuel shortages at certain service stations.
Pan Malaysian Bus Operators Association president Datuk Mohamad Ashfar Ali said the government's 150-litre cap per purchase forces some operators to make additional refuelling stops.
He said the situation was complicated by some stations lacking sufficient diesel supply and the fact that Subsidised Diesel Control Scheme (SKDS) fleet cards are tied to specific brands.
"If a bus is travelling from Johor to Penang and the operator's fleet card can only be used at Petron, the driver cannot refuel at Petronas or Shell.
"This means the driver will need to look for another Petron station, and this will affect travel time," he told the New Straits Times.
Ashfar said if a driver had no choice but to refuel at a station where the fleet card was not accepted, the operator would have to foot the bill.
Ashfar said that some members have reported shortages at certain stations rather than an overall diesel shortage.
The unsubsidised price for diesel is RM6.72 per litre in Peninsular Malaysia until April 15, compared with the RM2.15 per litre enjoyed by fleet card holders.
"Short-distance services are unlikely to be significantly affected, but long-haul routes will require more careful planning as they depend on higher diesel consumption and fixed schedules," he said.
Ashfar said that the monthly subsidised diesel quota of 6,000 litres per bus per month was sufficient for most operators.
"The challenge now is ensuring timely journeys, especially for long-haul trips. We hope the public understands these challenges."
Ashfar said PMBOA appreciates the efforts taken by the government to ensure adequate fuel supply and hopes that the 150-litre cap, which came into effect in April, will be temporary.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Netanyahu hails war success, says Iran's nuclear programme has been crushed
Article:
JERUSALEM: Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said on Saturday that the joint US-Israeli campaign against Iran had succeeded in "crushing" the Islamic republic's nuclear and ballistic missile programmes.
Netanyahu's comments came as Iranian and US officials held two rounds of face-to-face talks in Pakistan in a bid to end the Middle East war, with a third round expected later on Saturday evening or Sunday, Iranian state television reported.
"We have succeeded in crushing the nuclear programme, and crushing the missile programme," Netanyahu said in a televised statement, adding that the war against Teheran had also weakened Iran's leadership and its regional allies.
"We have reached a situation in which Iran no longer has a single functioning enrichment facility."
Netanyahu said the United States and Israel had prevented Iran from acquiring a nuclear bomb by launching a war in June 2025, followed by the current campaign that began on Feb 28.
He said the latest war was launched after intelligence indicated that the now deceased Iranian supreme leader Ali Khamenei had sought to expand the country's nuclear and missile programmes even after the June 2025 war.
"He sought to bury both missile production and nuclear production deep, deep beneath a mountain, in a way that even B-2 aircraft could not reach. Once again, we could not stand by. We acted," Netanyahu said.
"Most of its missile production capacity has disappeared. They still have missiles, they still have stockpiles, but it is shrinking."
He said there were "enormous achievements" in the war effort.
"They are reflected in this weakened regime, which is now even seeking a ceasefire," he said.
Netanyahu added that, for decades, Iran's leadership and its allies had threatened Israel.
"They wanted to strangle us, and (now) we are strangling them. They threatened us with annihilation, and now they are fighting for survival."
- ISRAELIS RALLY AGAINST WARS -
On Lebanon, Netanyahu said the country had approached Israel regarding a potential peace deal.
"In the past month, it has reached out several times to begin direct peace talks," Netanyahu said.
"I have given my approval, but on two conditions: we want the dismantling of Hizbollah's weapons, and we want a real peace agreement that will last for generations."
On Friday, Lebanon's presidency said that a meeting would be held with Israel in Washington next week to discuss a ceasefire in the Israel-Hizbollah war and the potential start of negotiations between the neighbours.
Hezbollah and Israeli forces have been clashing since March 2, two days after the start of the Iran war, following rocket fire by the Lebanese armed group at Israel in retaliation for the killing of Khamenei.
Since then, Israel has killed at least 2,020 people in Lebanon, including 248 women, 165 children and 85 medical and emergency personnel, according to the Lebanese health ministry.
Israel carried out its largest air attack this week on Lebanon since March 2, which it says left hundreds of Hizbollah fighters dead.
Even as Netanyahu spoke, around 800 Israeli protesters rallied in Tel Aviv on Saturday to protest against the wars in Iran and Lebanon, accusing Netanyahu of attempting to derail the ceasefire with Iran.
"In Bibi, we don't trust," read one banner, using Netanyahu's nickname, as protesters chanted: "No to endless wars, no to a government of death."
Protester Martin Goldberg said Netanyahu was not in favour of a ceasefire with Iran.
"What happened immediately after the ceasefire in Iran, Israel launched one of its largest attacks in Lebanon, which in my opinion, was an attempt to try and sabotage the ceasefire with Iran," the 61-year-old demonstrator told AFP.
"Israel is pretty openly saying that they're not interested in a ceasefire in Iran, and they don't want a peaceful solution in Iran."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Selangor waste management firm allays concerns over uncollected rubbish
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:   KDEB Waste Management Sdn Bhd has said  that despite temporary challenges with diesel supply, there are no changes to its operations.
The company told the
New Straits Times
that  residential and commercial waste were being collected on schedule across all 12 municipalities in Selangor, with no disruptions.
KDEB was responding to complaints about uncollected garbage, with many questioning if this was because of the diesel problem.
KDEB managing director Datuk Ramli Tahir said  despite some temporary challenges related to the supply of diesel, there had been no changes in its operations.
" However, KDEB has taken immediate mitigation steps to ensure there are sufficient assets and manpower.
"This is to ensure that waste collection services continue smoothly without prolonged disruptions."
He added that there had been no drop in collection routes, and all pickups are  on schedule.
"The waste collection frequency is maintained as usual according to established schedules."
Collection is carried out three times a week for residential areas, and daily for commercial areas, strata properties and bin centres.
"At present, 1,300 rubbish trucks are mobilised across Selangor. The number of trucks remains the same, without any reduction. We have also enhanced our monitoring to ensure smooth operations in all areas."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Higher diesel prices add to hauliers' burden
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:    The rise in diesel prices is beginning to take a toll on hauliers, forcing some to raise prices and causing  others to exhaust credit limits with oil companies.
Association of Malaysian Hauliers secretary-general Mohamad Azuan Masud said fuel   was a major operating cost, and while hauliers enjoyed subsidies, other costs, like  drivers' wages, engine oil, spare parts, tyres and maintenance, also weighed them down.
Azuan said hauliers transporting high-density cargo and perishable goods were particularly affected.
"Refrigerated transport requires additional energy consumption, and specialised equipment, such as side loader trailers, also incurs higher operational costs due to additional power requirements."
He added that some operators had adjusted their   rates to reflect increased costs.
"Based on feedback, many clients are aware of the current cost environment and have generally accepted these adjustments."
Beyond direct costs, Azuan said the diesel price, which has surged from RM3.12 per litre in early March to RM6.72 this week, has also affected credit arrangements with oil companies.
"Credit limits are typically based on the pump price rather than the subsidised price.
"This reduces the available credit, causing some operators to exhaust their limits much earlier, sometimes within a week, despite the usual 30-day credit term."
He said the subsidy adjustment was only reflected at the end of the month, and operators were often required to make early payments to continue using fleet cards, impacting cash flow.
"The diesel subsidy mechanism has provided some level of support, particularly in stabilising prices of essential goods for the public.
"However, the benefit is more pronounced for end consumers and cargo owners, rather than directly addressing operational challenges faced by hauliers."
He added that rising logistics costs would  contribute to a higher cost of living due to inflationary pressures.
"Continued government support measures will be important in managing these impacts."
Despite the challenges, Azuan said there had not been a significant change in delivery routes, frequency or operational efficiency.
Azuan said the association was concerned over the significant gap between the subsidised diesel price (RM2.15) and the current pump price (RM6.72), as it created a strong incentive for fuel diversion and cross-border smuggling.
"From an industry perspective, this not only undermines the integrity of the subsidy mechanism but also creates an uneven playing field.
"Legitimate operators who comply with regulations are placed at a disadvantage, while leakages in the system result in substantial fiscal losses to the government."
He added that while targeted subsidies were necessary, the mechanism must be continuously refined.
This would help to minimise leakages, ensure that benefits reach the intended sectors and avoid unintended consequences, such as market distortions and illicit activities, said Azuan.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Diesel price surge: Parents fear school bus fares will spike
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:   Parents of school-going children are increasingly worried about potential school bus fare hikes as diesel prices  spike.
War in the Middle East has disrupted global supply chains and pushed up fuel costs, which impact transport providers like school buses.
S. Kumar, who has two children attending  a primary school in Sepang, said he  pays RM180 per month for each child's bus fare from their home in Taman Seroja to the school, a distance of about 6.5km.
"When diesel was priced around RM3 a litre, fares were manageable.
"Now it's past RM6 and may go even higher. The school bus operator is already warning us of a fare increase.
"Just on Friday, the school bus driver told us he may need to increase the fares to RM220 or even higher  if prices continue to rise," said the 46-year-old father of three.
He added that the operator had complained of barely making any profit at current rates and possible losses if diesel prices increased further.
The price of diesel   in Malaysia has risen sharply in recent weeks to  RM6.72 per litre.
School bus operators are given a subsidy of RM200 a day to buy diesel.
Another parent, Nur Aisyah Rahman, 39, whose daughter studies at a secondary school in Dengkil, said she paid RM150 monthly for transport from Taman Permata Dengkil, approximately 4.2km away.
"It's quite a short distance, but I'm paying quite a lot because my husband and I work irregular hours.
"Previously, when diesel was subsidised, the bus fare was RM120. In March, the operator increased it by RM30.
"Now, we don't know what to expect after the latest price hike," she said.
"What worries me is not just one increase, but the possibility that it keeps going up every few months. Parents are already dealing with rising food prices, school expenses and daily costs.
"If transport costs also keep increasing, it becomes very difficult to manage.
"Some parents are talking about carpooling or sending their children themselves, but not everyone has that flexibility. Many of us rely on school buses because of our work commitments. If fares go too high, we will be forced to make difficult choices," said the senior sales executive.
Kuala Lumpur and Selangor School Bus Operators Association representative Khairul Fauzi said operators   were struggling to maintain  fares.
"It is becoming increasingly difficult to manage, but we also understand that some parents cannot afford higher fares," he said.
"Current diesel prices and fleet card limits are no longer practical for daily operations. The government must review this.
"Previously, with a RM200 fleet card limit, I could fill a full tank. Now RM200 is not enough, I used to get about 40 to 43 litres, but now it's only around 36 litres," he said.
He added that operators were now forced to make repeated trips to refuel, affecting efficiency and schedules.
"We have to keep going back and forth to petrol stations because diesel runs out quickly. It disrupts  pick-up schedules."
Khairul said  while the fleet card provided subsidised diesel at around RM2.15 per litre, the daily cap no longer reflected current market realities.
"The RM200 limit is no longer relevant with current diesel prices. Even though the subsidised rate still exists, the amount we can purchase is too limited."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Most traders absorb higher production costs to offload supplies
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:  Poultry farmers are selling their chickens below cost to keep stocks moving, said the Federation of Livestock Farmers Association.
Its adviser, Datuk Jeffrey Ng, said the Middle East crisis has driven up feed prices,  pushing production costs up by five to seven per cent.
Yet demand has softened, leaving farmers with excess supply.
"Because of market concern and sentiment, the demand for chicken and eggs is low," he said, adding that current prices are below production cost.
The mismatch has created a paradox across the market: retail prices appear to be rising, but farmers are offloading perishable goods at a loss as they cannot afford to hold stock.
In other segments of the produce sector,  traders say they are absorbing much of the pressure rather than passing it on.
Vegetable seller Kamol Cheng Thean described his margins have been wiped out by rising transport costs.
"After paying for lorry deliveries and capital, there's nothing left. No savings. No profit.
"Everything goes to paying the lorry," he said.
Others report similar strain. Prices for basic goods such as  tofu, bean sprouts and lemongrass are up, while more increases are expected.
[[nid:1415592]]
For hawkers, the strategy has been to hold prices steady, for now.
Federation of Malaysian  Hawkers and Traders Associations president Datuk Seri  Rosli Sulaiman said many are
limiting price increases to avoid driving customers away, even as profits shrink.
"If normally they could make RM100 a day, today it's down to RM80 or RM60.
"They absorb the cost. But we have to see how long they can hold out."
The pressure extends beyond food.
Packaging materials such as plastic bags and containers have also become more expensive, compounding the squeeze.
Upstream, transport disruptions are beginning to show. In Cameron Highlands, rising diesel costs have  pushed delivery costs from about RM150 to as much as RM500.
Cameron Highlands Vegetable Growers Association deputy president Lau Weng Soow said some farmers have scaled back production as they can't afford the transport costs.
Other sectors report similar strategies:    holding prices where possible, cutting costs where necessary and waiting to see how long the current pressures will last. But industry players warn that this balancing act may not hold indefinitely.
Agriculture and Food Security Minister Datuk Seri Mohamad Sabu said the ministry would continue to hold engagement sessions to find the best solutions to reduce pressure on farmers, industry players and   consumers.
"We fully understand the mounting pressures and challenges  faced by livestock farmers amid ongoing industry concerns."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Diesel surge hurting businesses
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:       Malaysian supply chains are in a bind as    war in the Middle East drives up  diesel prices.
From farmers and traders to transport operators, players in various sectors of the economy  are treading carefully as operating costs climb while pricing power remains limited, squeezing margins at every level.
Many businesses are caught in a delicate balancing act: while fuel and logistics costs continue to rise, passing these increases on to consumers risks dampening already fragile demand.
Industry players warn that prolonged volatility could disrupt supply chains and place further strain on livelihoods.
Amid the pressure, stakeholders are increasingly urging Putrajaya to step in with targeted measures to cushion the impact.
Among them is Kedah Umno liaison committee chairman Datuk Seri Mahdzir Khalid, who has called for a comprehensive mechanism to support padi farmers facing mounting operational expenses.
He said the sharp rise in diesel prices — now at RM6.72 per litre — has significantly driven up cultivation costs, particularly for machinery use, with many operators unable to access subsidised fuel through fleet card facilities.
"As an example, the cost of ploughing fields was previously around RM160 for two rounds. However, the cost has now increased to more than RM300," he said, adding that farmers must also contend with fertiliser and transportation costs.
Mahdzir urged the government, particularly the Finance Ministry, to review the entire padi production chain to ensure farmers' margins are protected and the sector remains sustainable.
Consumers, including hawkers, traders and farmers told the
New Straits Times
that they are cautious about the prices of foodstuff and waiting to see how long the current pressures will last.
Checks by the NST in multiple locations suggest that fears of a widespread diesel shortage may be overstated, despite viral claims on social media.
Across Penang, including Batu Ferringhi, Tanjung Bungah and Balik Pulau, diesel supply was available at all stations visited.
Similar observations were made in Johor Baru and Iskandar Puteri, where no shortages were reported.
In Kelantan, checks at four stations  in Pengkalan Kubor, Tumpat, found diesel supply to be stable, with no shortages reported along the border stretch.
In the Klang Valley, inspections along Jalan Klang Lama and in Bangsar also found fuel to be readily available at all stations surveyed.
However, isolated disruptions were observed in Segambut, where a station had run out of diesel, with staff indicating that supplies were expected to be replenished later in the day.
In Wangsa Maju, a station in Sri Rampai reported that diesel had run out the previous day but had since been restocked.
Overall, checks at more than 20 stations found only a small number were temporarily affected, pointing to sporadic supply issues rather than a systemic shortage.
Oil companies have attributed such instances to short-term spikes in demand amid global supply volatility, noting that efforts are ongoing to ensure supply continuity.
Industry groups echoed this view, saying they have not observed any widespread disruption, even as concerns persist over rising fuel costs and their knock-on effects across the economy.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran threatens 'severe' response to US Navy ships in Hormuz
Article:
TEHERAN: Iran's Revolutionary Guards threatened to deal "severely" with any military vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz, state TV reported Sunday, after the US Central Command said two US Navy warships had transited the strategic waterway to clear mines laid by Tehran.
"Any attempt by military vessels to pass through the Strait of Hormuz will be dealt with severely. The IRGC Navy has full authority to manage the Strait of Hormuz intelligently," the Guards' Navy Command said, according to state broadcaster IRIB, adding passage of the strait would only be "granted to civilian vessels under specific conditions."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US warships enter Hormuz to clear mines as tensions with Iran escalate
Article:
WASHINGTON: Two US Navy warships transited the Strait of Hormuz to begin clearing Iranian-laid mines, US Central Command said Saturday – a claim Teheran denied as the Revolutionary Guards threatened to deal "severely" with military vessels crossing the strategic waterway.
The announcement of the first such transit since the US-Israeli war with Iran began came shortly after President Donald Trump said Washington had started "clearing out" the strait, through which a fifth of the world's crude oil passes.
"Today, we began the process of establishing a new passage and we will share this safe pathway with the maritime industry soon to encourage the free flow of commerce," said CENTCOM commander Admiral Brad Cooper.
The USS Frank E. Peterson and the USS Michael Murphy are the guided-missile destroyers involved in the operation, but CENTCOM said that "additional US forces including underwater drones" could join the effort in coming days.
Iran "strongly rejected" Washington's claims that US vessels entered the strait, military spokesperson Ebrahim Zolfaghari told state TV.
"The initiative for the passage of any vessel lies with the armed forces of the Islamic Republic of Iran," he was quoted as saying.
Iranian state broadcaster IRIB later quoted the Revolutionary Guards' Navy Command as saying: "Any attempt by military vessels to pass through the Strait of Hormuz will be dealt with severely."
It added that passage of the strait would only be "granted to civilian vessels under specific conditions."
Earlier Saturday, Trump said in a social media post that the United States was "starting the process of clearing out the Strait of Hormuz."
He called it "a favour" to countries such as China, Japan and France that "don't have the Courage or Will to do this work themselves."
Trump insisted that Iran is "LOSING BIG!" in the conflict, while acknowledging that Iranian mines in the strategic strait still pose a threat.
"The only thing they have going is the threat that a ship may 'bunk' into one of their sea mines," Trump wrote.
The key shipping lane off the coast of Iran has been virtually blocked by Teheran since the United States and Israel started bombing Iran on Feb 28, though reopening the strait was ostensibly a condition of the shaky ceasefire put in place earlier this week.
Senior Iranian and American officials held face-to-face negotiations in Pakistan on Saturday in a bid to bring an end to a conflict that has plunged the Middle East into violence and sent shockwaves through the world economy.
In an earlier post, Trump said empty tankers were headed to the United States from around the world to purchase oil, without providing details.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US-Iran talks enter second day amid  Strait of Hormuz tensions
Article:
ISLAMABAD: Top-level peace talks between the United States and Iran entered a second day early Sunday, as Washington piled pressure by saying it had sent minesweeping ships through the vital Strait of Hormuz.
In the Pakistani capital Islamabad, Vice President JD Vance on Saturday became the highest-ranking American to meet directly with Iranian officials since the 1979 Islamic revolution, days after the United States and Israel halted their war that had plunged the Middle East and global economy into tumult.
The White House said talks extended beyond midnight and Iranian media accused the United States of making "excessive demands" on the Strait of Hormuz, through which one-fifth of the world's oil transits.
Donald Trump said that "deep negotiations" were underway but in a combative interaction with reporters Saturday, the president contended he did not care about the outcome, insisting the United States had already triumphed on the battlefield by killing Iranian leaders and destroying key military infrastructure.
"Whether we make a deal or not makes no difference to me. The reason is because we've won," Trump said.
A Pakistani official told AFP talks were "progressing in the right direction."
"I can say that discussions are moving positively and the overall atmosphere is cordial," said the official, speaking on condition of anonymity.
In a break with precedent, Iranian and US officials were meeting directly, alongside Pakistan, and not speaking through mediators who shuttled between rooms.
Iran had sought the presence of Vance due to his top position and his reported initial opposition to the war.
Iran was amid negotiations on its nuclear programme in February with Trump's real-estate friend Steve Witkoff and son-in-law Jared Kushner only to see the United States and Israel to launch their attack that began with the killing of Iran's long-ruling supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
Both Kushner and Witkoff joined Vance. The 70-strong Iranian delegation was led by Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, the powerful speaker of parliament, and included Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi.
- US SAYS TARGETING MINES -
Iran quickly exercised leverage after being attacked by exerting control over the Strait of Hormuz, sending global oil prices soaring and piling political pressure on Trump as Americans complained of rising costs at the pump.
The US military said Saturday that two Navy warships transited through the strait to begin clearing it of mines and ensure it is a "safe pathway" for tankers.
The Iranian military denied that any American warships had entered the waterway and threatened to respond if they do so.
"Any attempt by military vessels to pass through the Strait of Hormuz will be dealt with severely," the Revolutionary Guards' Naval Command said in a statement, according to state broadcaster IRIB.
It said that Iranian promises of safe passage during a two-week ceasefire applied only to "civilian vessels under specific conditions."
The United States is heavily impacted by soaring oil prices on global markets but imports less directly from the Gulf than many of its European allies – which Trump has berated for not joining a war that they were not consulted about beforehand.
"We'll open up the strait even though we don't use it, because we have a lot of other countries in the world that do use it that are either afraid or weak or cheap," Trump said.
Iran demands for any agreement to end the war include unfreezing sanctioned Iranian assets and ending Israel's war against Hizbollah in Lebanon, which Vance has said will not be up for discussion in Islamabad.
- MUTUAL SUSPICION -
Ghalibaf, speaking shortly after landing in Pakistan, made clear that Iran remained highly suspicious of the United States.
"Our experience in negotiating with the Americans has always been met with failure and broken promises," Ghalibaf said.
Vance said before leaving the United States that if Iran was "willing to negotiate in good faith, we're certainly willing to extend the open hand."
But he said the United States would not be receptive "if they're going to try to play us."
Experts said Iran's delegation showed it was serious about leaving Pakistan with a deal.
"The size, seniority and breadth of the Iranian delegation...signal both Teharan's sincerity in these negotiations and its expectations and confidence," said Trita Parsi, executive vice president of the Quincy Institute for Responsible Statecraft and author of a book on US-Iran diplomacy.
Israel has insisted that the ceasefire does not affect its invasion of Lebanon, where Israel has carried out massive strikes and sent troops in response to fire from Hizbollah, the Iranian-backed Shia Muslim movement.
Lebanese authorities said Israeli strikes on the country's south on Saturday killed 18 people, bringing the total death toll from Israeli strikes since the war broke out past 2,000.
Israel and Lebanon will hold their own talks next week in Washington. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu in a televised statement Saturday said he wanted a peace deal with Lebanon that "will last for generations."
But Israel has ruled out a ceasefire with Hizbollah, signalling it will instead seek to pressure the historically weak central government in Beirut.
- ISLAMABAD SEALED OFF -
Netanyahu said the joint US-Israeli campaign had already succeeded in "crushing" Iran's nuclear and ballistic missile programmes.
Iran has repeatedly denied seeking an atomic bomb and the UN nuclear watchdog reported no imminent threat of Teharan going nuclear.
Pakistan sealed off much of Islamabad to ensure high security for the talks.
Pakistan is especially sensitive to developments in neighbouring Iran and is also keen to court Trump and counter a US tilt in recent years towards Pakistan's rival India.
In Teharan, a 30-year-old resident told AFP he was sceptical negotiations would be successful, describing most of what Trump says as "pure noise and nonsense."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Grief as soldier begins final journey home after alleged assault in Bentong camp
Article:
TEMERLOH: The hearse carrying the remains of soldier Muhammad Amirul Raziq Rosafindi, who died 12 days after being placed on life support following an alleged assault at a military camp in Bentong on March 30, has departed for his hometown in Negri Sembilan.
The armed forces hearse left Kampung Paya Luas mosque at 11.45pm after the body was bathed and shrouded there.
A police mobile patrol vehicle was assigned to escort the hearse convoy, along with vehicles carrying Amirul Raziq's family members and relatives, to Gemas.
The remains will be taken to Felda Sungai Kelamah mosque for funeral prayers before burial at Felda Sungai Kelamah Muslim cemetery in Gemas.
Earlier, the body was brought to the mosque at 10.10pm after a post-mortem, which began at 5pm at the forensic unit of Sultan Haji Ahmad Shah Hospital (Hoshas) here, and was completed at 9.55pm.
Military officers and personnel gathered at the mosque to pay their last respects before the coffin, draped with the Jalur Gemilang, was carried by nine military pallbearers into the hearse.
Amirul Raziq's father, Rosafindi Tambi Chik, 49, and other family members were grief-stricken as they watched the coffin being carried into the hearse.
They were then escorted into waiting vehicles that followed the hearse, while some relatives declined interviews.
Amirul Raziq, 24,
died at 9.40am
yesterday after battling for his life at Hoshas following the alleged
assault
at the 25th Battalion of the Royal Malay Regiment camp in Bentong on March 30.
He was allegedly assaulted by a junior non-commissioned officer at the camp.
The accused, Corporal Sukry Tasu,
pleaded not guilty
at the Bentong magistrate's court on April 6 to seven charges of assaulting a fellow personnel.
He was charged with causing grievous hurt and voluntarily causing hurt, and was remanded at Bentong Prison after failing to post bail.
Following Amirul Raziq's death, Bentong police chief Superintendent Zaiham Mohd Kahar said the case is now being investigated as
murder
under Section 302 of the Penal Code.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Govt vows action if criminal elements found in soldier's death
Article:
JELEBU: The government will not compromise if there are criminal elements in the death of army personnel, Private Muhammad Amirul Raziq Rosafindi, who is believed to have been beaten by a corporal last month.
Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi said the government viewed the matter seriously and is committed to ensuring that justice is served and that such incidents do not recur in the future.
"Disciplinary action cannot be taken arbitrarily, especially when it involves physical abuse.
"All parties must comply with the standard operating procedures (SOP) that have been set and not use methods outside the existing guidelines," he told reporters after the Jelebu parliamentary constituency Aidilfitri Open House, last night.
Also present was Jelebu member of parliament and Negri Sembilan Senior Exco Datuk Seri Jalaluddin Alias.
The deceased Muhammad Amirul, 24, was previously reported to be in a coma and receiving intensive care at Sultan Haji Ahmad Shah Hospital (HoSHAS), Temerloh following serious injuries sustained following a beating incident at the 25th Battalion Camp of the Royal Malay Regiment (RAMD), Bentong on March 30.
The incident was reported to have been caused by the actions of a 37-year-old Junior Non-Commissioned Officer (PTT) who was accused of using force due to dissatisfaction with the assigned duty.
Meanwhile at Gemas, the victim's close friend, Muhammad Afham Hafidz Adzhar, 24, described the deceased as a friendly, cheerful and very likeable person.
Muhammad Afham Hafidz Adzhar, 24, said he and other friends were shocked when they heard the news of what had befallen their close friend, whom they had known since the age of 16.
"I am saddened that I did not have time to visit the deceased in the hospital.
"The last time we were in contact was before Hari Raya, and at that time he informed me that he was not feeling well, such as having a fever," he said when met by reporters at the Felda Sungai Kelamah Kariah Mosque, Gemas, here, last night.
He said the deceased had a deep interest in the military and aspired to join the commando force, but was unsuccessful.
The remains of Private Muhammad Amirul will be buried early Sunday morning at the Felda Sungai Kelamah Cemetery, Gemas.
He
died at a hospital in Temerloh
, Pahang, yesterday, 12 days after the assault by corporal.
Police said the case would be
reclassified as murder
.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 329,000 barrels of diesel sent to Philippines not from Malaysia - MTEN
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The National Economic Action Council (MTEN) has confirmed that the 329,000 barrels of diesel recently delivered to the Philippines did not originate from Malaysia, contrary to international media reports.
In a statement, MTEN's crisis management team advised the public against speculation and urged reliance on official government sources.
Earlier, the Philippine News Agency reported that about 329,000 barrels, or 52.311 million litres, of diesel from Malaysia had been shipped to the Philippines to boost the country's fuel supply amid global market uncertainties.
According to the report, the shipment was part of efforts by the Philippine government to strengthen domestic supply, following an earlier delivery of 142,000 barrels, or 22.578 million litres, from Japan on March 26.
In response, Petroliam Nasional Bhd (Petronas)
denied any involvement
in the reported diesel supplies to the Philippines.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US warships transit Strait of Hormuz - reports
Article:
WASHINGTON: Two US warships have reportedly passed through the Strait of Hormuz, the first such transit since the war with Iran began, as President Donald Trump said Saturday that the United States had started "clearing out" the strategic waterway.
The US Navy guided-missile destroyers passed through the strait with no issues reported, The Wall Street Journal reported, citing three US officials.
The operation was not coordinated with authorities in Tehran, US media outlet Axios said.
"We're now starting the process of clearing out the Strait of Hormuz," Trump said on his Truth Social platform, calling it "a favor" to countries such as China, Japan and France that "don't have the Courage or Will to do this work themselves."
He insisted that Iran is "LOSING BIG!" in the conflict, while acknowledging that Iranian mines in the strategic strait – through which a fifth of the world's crude passes – still pose a threat.
"The only thing they have going is the threat that a ship may 'bunk' into one of their sea mines," Trump wrote.
US officials did not immediately respond to AFP requests for comments about the reports.
The key shipping lane off the coast of Iran has been virtually blocked by Teheran since the United States and Israel started bombing Iran on February 28, though reopening the strait was ostensibly a condition of the shaky ceasefire put in place earlier this week.
Senior Iranian and American officials began negotiations in Pakistan on Saturday, Iranian media reported, in a bid to bring to an end a conflict that has plunged the Middle East into violence and sent shockwaves through the world economy.
In an earlier post, Trump said that empty tankers were headed to the United States from around the world to purchase oil, without providing details. — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran-US talks in Islamabad enter ex-expert level phase
Article:
ISTANBUL: Talks between Iran and the US in Islamabad have entered the expert-level stage, with economic, military, legal and nuclear committees joining the process, Iran's government said in a post on the US social media company X.
According to Anadolu Ajansi, the negotiations are taking place at a hotel in the Pakistani capital, where technical discussions are focused on finalising key issues.
The statement said the current phase follows intensive consultations and initial progress on major points, including the release of Iranian assets and developments related to the situation in Lebanon.
Iran has made the release of its frozen assets a central demand, with the US side agreeing in principle, according to the statement.
Iranian media had previously reported that discussions would
focus on the release of frozen assets
and developments in
Lebanon
, including continued strikes in southern areas despite a broader reduction in hostilities.
Iran's Parliament Speaker Bagher Qalibaf and his team, including Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi and deputy to the National Security Council, Ali Bagheri Kani, arrived in Islamabad earlier on Saturday.
Pakistan is hosting the landmark negotiations, seen as the most significant since 1979, aimed at ending the broader regional conflict amid a fragile two-week ceasefire brokered earlier this week.
– Bernama-Anadolu
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 8,000 yaba pills seized in Kelantan raid
Article:
PASIR MAS: The General Operations Force (GOF) seized 8,000 yaba pills worth about RM80,000 in an operation at Kampung Kubang Gadong yesterday.
GOF Southeast Brigade commander Senior Assistant Commissioner Ahmad Radzi Hussain said no arrests were made in the 3pm operation.
He said the seizure was made under Op Taring Wawasan Kelantan by the Strike Force of the Ninth Battalion based in Kuala Terengganu.
He said the raid was carried out following intelligence on migrant smuggling activities in the area.
"During surveillance, the team found a blue sling bag left on the ground in an oil palm plantation.
"Observation for about 30 minutes from a distance of 50m showed no one came forward to claim it," he said.
Ahmad Radzi said inspection of the bag led to the discovery of 8,000 yaba pills believed to be for local distribution.
All seized items were taken to the Pasir Mas police Narcotics Criminal Investigation Division for further action.
The case is being investigated under Section 39B of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Op Samseng Jalanan: Police seize 117 motorcycles, detain 4 riders
Article:
IPOH: A total of 117 motorcycles were seized at the Simpang Lawin traffic lights, Jalan Kuala Kangsar–Gerik near Kuala Kangsar, yesterday in Op Samseng Jalanan (Street Thugs Operation).
Kuala Kangsar district police chief assistant commissioner  Heisham Harun said the operation was carried out starting at 10pm with the cooperation of the Traffic Enforcement Investigation Department of the Perak police contingent headquarters and the Road Transport Department (RTD).
"As a result of intelligence and operations, the police arrested four motorcyclists under Section 42 (1) of the Road Transport Act 1987.
"In addition, a total of 117 motorcycles were seized under Section 64 of the Road Transport Act 1987," he said in a statement today.
He said a total of 252 summonses were issued by the police while RTD issued 119 summons notices against all motorcyclists and car drivers for various traffic offences and motorcycle modifications.
He said a total of 12 motorcyclists had undergone urine tests and two of them were found positive for methamphetamine and amphetamine.
Heisham said both were being investigated under Section 15 (1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952 by the Narcotics Criminal Investigation Division of the Kuala Kangsar district police headquarters.
"The police would like to advise the public to stay away from the street thug culture and "channel information regarding street gang activities to the police for further action," he said.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Petronas denies link to diesel supply to Philippines
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Petroliam Nasional Bhd (Petronas) has denied any involvement in reported diesel supplies to the Philippines.
The national oil company said it has not entered into any such arrangement with any party in the country.
"Petronas refers to recent media reports on diesel supplies from Malaysia to the Philippines.
"The company wishes to clarify that it has not entered into any such arrangement with any party in the Philippines," it said in a statement today.
The company said its utmost priority remains ensuring a reliable and continuous fuel supply for Malaysia, in close coordination with the government and relevant stakeholders.
Earlier today, the Philippine News Agency (PNA), the Philippine government's newswire service, reported that about 329,000 barrels, or 52.311 million litres, of diesel from Malaysia had been delivered to the country.
Its Department of Energy (DOE) Secretary Sharon Garin said the arrival of the second shipment reflects the government's ongoing efforts to boost domestic supply, as tensions in the Gulf region continue to escalate.
"This latest shipment from Malaysia further strengthens our supply position at a time when external risks remain, and the situation in the Middle East continues to evolve," Garin was reported as saying.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Stabbed for saying no: Is online misogyny fuelling violence in Brazil?
Article:
Anisio Rosa, 20, politely turned down the man from her gym who kept sending her flowers and chocolates.
A month later, he burst into her home and stabbed her around 50 times with a pocket knife.
Her mother, Jaderluce Anisio de Oliveira, 53, was confronted by the scene in February after returning early to their home in Sao Goncalo, a city across the bay from Rio de Janeiro.
"He just kept stabbing her, over and over again," Oliveira said.
"I pulled him off her. My entire living room was covered in blood."
As Alana emerged from an induced coma and recovered from multiple surgeries in the following weeks, videos on TikTok went viral in Brazil of men beating and stabbing mannequins with the slogan: "Training in case she says 'no'."
Oliveira said that her daughter's attacker "followed this specific type of content" on social media.
In Brazil, alarm is rising about a surge in misogynistic "Red Pill" content online, which experts warn may be contributing to crimes against women in a country already struggling with high rates of gender-based violence.
In January, after a 17-year-old girl was allegedly gang-raped by five teenagers in Rio, one of the suspects turned himself in to police wearing a T-shirt that read "Regret Nothing",  a phrase linked to prominent "Red Pill" influencers worldwide.
Two months later, a military policeman was arrested for allegedly shooting his wife, who wanted a divorce.
In text messages published by local media, he describes himself as an "alpha male", saying she should be an "obedient, submissive, beta female".
Daniel Cara, a professor at the University of Sao Paulo  who has researched "Red Pill" culture, said it both "legitimises and encourages" violence against women.
Brazil recorded 1,568 murders of women in 2025, the highest number since femicide  became a crime a decade ago.
President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva said recently that "men are becoming increasingly inhuman and violent".
Estela Bezerra, the head of Brazil's office on violence against women, said she believes online misogyny plays a big role.
"This 'Red Pill' content is, fundamentally, hate speech. It propagates a set of values that threatens to drag our society... back into an era of barbarism."
The term comes from the 1999 hit film
The Matrix
, in which taking a red pill reveals a hidden and often uncomfortable truth.
A study by the Federal University of Rio de Janeiro showed that 123 YouTube channels containing hate speech and promoting control over women had 23 million subscribers in March, an 18 per cent rise in two years. Once hidden in the dark recesses of the web, this content is now easy to find.
"This gives rise to a phenomenon that goes beyond mere desensitisation: it is the dehumanisation of an entire gender," said Flavio Rolim, head of the police's cyber hate crime unit.
In February,  police targeted Brazilian men involved in an international network who would drug and rape women and share videos of the abuse.
Some conservative commentators argue the "Red Pill" movement is primarily about male self-improvement and has no link to femicide.
Experts are particularly concerned about how this content has seeped into the algorithms of young people.
Rolim said the police had found groups of 15- or 16-year-olds in chat groups saying: "Why would I date a girl when I can just rape her?"
At a school in Rio de Janeiro, Ana Elizabeth Barcelos Barbosa, 13,  said that influencers pushing the idea that "a woman's sole purpose is to serve men" is eroding girls' self-esteem.
Growing concern over the phenomenon has led to a flurry of recent legislative proposals.
Lawmaker Reimont Luiz Otoni Santa Barbara has introduced the "Red Pill Bill", aimed at criminalising content that he says promotes violence against women. Another bill approved by the Senate last month aims to classify misogyny as a crime similar to racism.
The writer is from AFP
The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Law enforcement must keep up as organised crime evolves
Article:
THE mafia is the most famous example of  a structured and hierarchical organised crime network that operates much like a business.
Mafia groups rely on management systems, strategic planning and territorial control in the conduct of illegal activities.
Their crimes include extortion, gambling, loan sharking and money laundering.  Violence and  murder are used to enforce authority, eliminate rivals and maintain internal discipline.
The  mafias' roots can be traced back to the late 19th and early 20th centuries, when Sicilian immigrants in cities like New York, Chicago, Detroit and Boston formed organised groups for protection and survival.
Among the most well-known Italian-American  mafias are the Genovese, Gambino and Lucchese crime families, which form part of the Five Families of La Cosa Nostra.
Each family is headed by a  boss, or don,   while the underboss, often a son groomed to  succeed the  father, is second in command.
The No. 3 figure is an adviser and confidant, and beneath him, captains or
capo
s oversee the lowest-ranking members, known as soldiers.
Efforts to dismantle  mafia operations in early 20th-century New York were often unsuccessful due to widespread corruption. Many law enforcement officers, public officials and even members of the judiciary were on the take.
Mafia leaders also insulated themselves from prosecution by delegating criminal acts to subordinates, making it difficult to trace responsibility.
Figures such as Al Capone and Carlo Gambino exemplified this strategy.
Their wealth enabled them to hire top legal representation, exploit legal loopholes and leverage corrupt connections to avoid conviction.
A shift in strategy saw authorities move away from prosecuting violent offences  and instead target financial crimes.
In 1931, Capone was convicted of tax evasion and sentenced to 11 years in prison, demonstrating how financial investigation can succeed where traditional methods fail, helping to lay the foundation for forensic accounting.
From the 1950s to the 1970s, increased law enforcement pressure and legal innovations, such as the Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organisations Act  enabled authorities to prosecute entire criminal organisations rather than just individuals.
In response,  mafia groups adapted by becoming more discreet and investing in legitimate industries, such as construction, transportation and waste management to launder money and avoid scrutiny.
Since the 1980s, organised crime has evolved  into decentralised  global networks.
Exploiting globalisation, technology and complex financial systems, these groups operate  across borders in  drug trafficking, cybercrime, fraud and human trafficking.
Hybrid gangs from Russia, China  and Mexico are often supported by professionals, such as lawyers and accountants, and use shell companies  for operations.
Financial deregulation  also aids them, allowing money laundering through placement, layering and integration.
Reports show that even Finland, which is regarded as a country low in corruption, has about 100 organised crime groups with growing networks and foreign influence.
Italy's major groups, apart from the Sicilian Mafia,  are the  'Ndrangheta and Camorra. They have tens of thousands of members, making the country a key centre of European organised crime.
'Ndrangheta  operates in dozens of countries and is deeply embedded in legitimate economies.
Modern law enforcement agencies, including the Federal Bureau of Investigation, report that mafia-related activities today include securities fraud, illegal gambling, online betting and sophisticated scams.
Effective prevention requires a multifaceted approach: adaptive legal frameworks, strong governance, vigilant enforcement, international cooperation and proactive oversight.
By combining transparency, collaboration and innovative strategies, societies can limit the influence of organised crime and safeguard institutions, communities and the rule of law.
Modern strategies such as forensic accounting, AI-driven investigations and intelligence-sharing support these efforts.
The Anti-Money Laundering Act  is central, allowing authorities to seize assets and prosecute financial crimes, though its broad powers require careful oversight to prevent misuse.
In conclusion, organised crime has shifted from rigid, violent local gangs to flexible, global, and tech-savvy networks.
Although violence is less visible, the goal remains profit, now pursued through cybercrime, fraud and sophisticated money laundering.
The writer is Professor of Criminology and Criminal Justice at HELP University
The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NST Leader: Reliance on EPF Flexible Account exposes a deep cost-of-living crisis
Article:
THE restructuring of the Employees' Provident Fund (EPF) in May 2024 introduced Akaun Fleksibel (Account 3), effectively transforming a minor portion of retirement savings into a virtual "automated teller machine" for contributors under 55 years old.
This new flexibility, which allows withdrawals of a minimum of RM50, was a direct response to widespread pressure from Malaysians grappling with a rising cost of living.
The massive uptake of Account 3 exposes the depth of this economic strain. Economists estimate RM16.6 billion had been withdrawn from the account up to June last year. Crucially, a study by EPF revealed that the vast majority — 70.3 per cent of five million contributors — used the funds for spending on daily necessities, including food and basic goods.
This trend reflects persistent inflationary pressures in sectors like food, transport, utilities and housing, which consumers feel intensely despite the official Consumer Price Index reporting a moderate inflation rate of 1.4 per cent in 2025.
Under the new structure, only 10 per cent of monthly contributions are allocated to Akaun Fleksibel, while 75 per cent goes to the Retirement Account (Account 1) and 15 per cent to the Well-Being Account (Account 2). This allocation is a hurdle for workers earning the minimum wage of RM1,700 a month, as about only RM39 of the monthly employee and employer contributions ends up in Account 3. This is well short of the minimum RM50 withdrawal amount, forcing low-wage workers to wait until their funds accumulate.
Given that the majority of those making frequent withdrawals are from the B40 group, the over-reliance on Account 3 signals both stagnant wages and a critical lack of an adequate social safety net. While Account 3 offers short-term relief for emergencies, debt avoidance or income shocks, the long-term trade-off is a compromise of retirement security and compounded old-age poverty.
Experts caution that Account 3 should be regarded as a compromise — a way to provide emergency access — rather than an expansion of the social safety net itself. To reduce this damaging reliance on retirement savings, the government should prioritise direct fiscal injections to ease immediate financial burdens.
By acknowledging that inflation is a continuous dilemma, the realistic objective for the government is to keep price volatility moderate and predictable, thereby ensuring growth, fiscal sustainability and a reduced necessity for citizens to dip into their essential retirement funds.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Gaza death toll rises to 72,328 since October 2023
Article:
RAMALLAH: The death toll from the Israeli aggression on the Gaza Strip has reached 72,328, with 172,184 injured, since the start of the assault on Oct 7, 2023, medical sources in Gaza reported.
According to Palestine News and Information Agency (WAFA),  hospitals in Gaza received the bodies of 11 Palestinians, along with 25 injured, in the past 24 hours.
The number of fatalities since the ceasefire on Oct 11 has risen to 749, with more than 2,082 injuries reported.
The bodies of 759 Palestinians have been retrieved over the same period.
Thousands of victims remain trapped under collapsed buildings or on the streets.
However, emergency responders are struggling to reach them due to a lack of essential equipment for debris removal and rescue operations.
– Bernama-Wafa
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Buffets, baristas, but no briefings: Journalists frozen out of Iran talks
Article:
THEY came from around the world: hundreds of journalists rushed to Islamabad's flagship convention centre – converted into a media hub by Pakistani authorities for landmark talks between the United States and Iran to end the war in the Middle East.
But as negotiations began behind closed doors half a kilometre away, all the world's media could do was wait – and sip on an expertly brewed coffee while listening to live eastern folk music.
Even the lattes had a tagline: "Brewed for Peace."
Branding was a big part of the event, with Pakistan dubbing the process the "Islamabad Talks", and plastering a logo combining the Pakistani, US and Iranian flags all over the city.
Early Saturday, an overcast Islamabad felt like a ghost town, with almost no civilian traffic on its wide avenues.
The few vehicles that moved were driven by armed, uniformed personnel who patrolled the city.
Some journalists, accredited by the information ministry, were nonetheless held up for about an hour at a checkpoint outside the venue as a convoy of VIPs swept past.
Inside, they found lavish surroundings, wedding-banquet style spreads of biryani, kebabs and gulab jamun, along with gourmet coffee blended from Brazilian and Ethiopian beans.
"We did the branding ('Brewed for Peace') just for the day," one person manning the stall said.
Outside the hall, musicians played folk songs on a small stage.
"I'm a professional musician, and also teach music," Amir Hussain Khan, a sitar player, told AFP.
Staff had reserved seating with a clear view of the massive main stage for US media, directing correspondents from other outlets elsewhere.
Iranian media went to the opposite side of the hall.
"I'm bored out of my mind," one journalist told AFP, declining to be named.
Another offered a similar assessment: "There's not much to do here."
Updates did not begin to arrive until after about 2pm (0900 GMT), hours after US Vice President JD Vance touched down in the capital.
[[nid:1415522]]
On the large screen dominating the cavernous Jinnah Convention Centre, state television aired footage of his arrival and reception by Pakistani officials, prominently including army chief Field Marshal Asim Munir.
When updates did come, they arrived not from media briefings – as would be the norm at an event of this scale – but from press statements released via WhatsApp.
The foreign ministry said a "high-powered" US delegation, led by Vance and including Special Envoy Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner, had been received by Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar, who commended Washington's "commitment to achieving lasting regional and global peace."
Hours later, Pakistan's government said Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif had met Vance, expressing hope the talks would serve as "a stepping stone toward durable peace in the region."
Not exactly the kind of gripping, headline-grabbing quotes many of the journalists in the hall had flown thousands of miles for.
The convention centre offered high-speed wireless internet that AFP clocked at more than 150 megabits per second, far above Pakistan's national average of 25 Mbps, according to Speedtest.net figures from February 2026.
The gesture was not lost on reporters.
"They say they have facilitated the media. No doubt they have given 5G internet speeds," said journalist Nadir Guramani.
"But media teams deputed inside Jinnah Convention Centre do not know what's happening outside."
Security measures added to the surreal atmosphere. An AFP journalist was told the coffee could not be taken into the main hall.
"Foreign media is here, and they are watching," a guard said cryptically.
By sunset, the "Islamabad Talks" had produced press releases, a memorable food spread and impressively fast internet in a country riven with tech challenges.
Whether they produced anything more substantive remained, for those inside the hall, just beyond their reach.
* The writer is from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: S'wak fire dept put out 54ha of bushfires in Bintulu, Matu-Daro
Article:
SIBU: The Sarawak Fire and Rescue Department put out 54ha of bushfires in several areas in Bintulu and Matu-Daro.
A department operations centre spokesman said that based on updates as of 7pm, the largest fire under control is along Jalan Matu-Igan near the Igan Bridge in Matu, with 20ha out of 35ha being put out.
"The 14ha bushfire behind the Marine Jepak Flats, Bintulu is almost completely put out, with 13ha put out.
"The 13ha bushfire along Jalan Kampung Kut–Kampung Saai in Pulau Bruit, Daro has been successfully put out after eight days of firefighting," they said in a statement tonight.
Also, the 4ha bushfires along Jalan Bruan–Tutus in Matu and in Kampung Penasu, Daro have also been put out.
"The department's air unit's Mi-17i aircraft conducted 10 water bombing sessions to put out the fire along Jalan Matu-Igan," they said, adding that firefighting operations will resume tomorrow in affected areas.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Russia-Ukraine 32-hour Easter ceasefire takes effect
Article:
MOSCOW: Russia's Defence Ministry announced on Saturday that a 32-hour ceasefire with Ukraine came into force at 4pm Moscow time (1300 GMT), Anadolu Ajansi reported.
The ceasefire was announced on Thursday by Russian President Vladimir Putin, who ordered the military command to prepare for its implementation.
Putin warned that if Ukraine undertakes a provocation, Moscow would respond.
Hours before the ceasefire came into force, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said Kyiv would join the initiative and act "in a mirror manner".
"Ukraine will observe the silence regime and will act strictly in a mirror manner.
"The absence of Russian strikes in the air, on land and at sea will mean the absence of our responses," he said on Telegram.
Zelenskyy asserted that the Ukrainian army was ready for any developments on the front.
"Ukraine has repeatedly proposed various ceasefire formats to Russia, and we believe that Easter should be a time of silence and security.
"A ceasefire for Easter could also be the beginning of real movement towards peace – our side has made a corresponding proposal," he said.
Zelenskyy said he discussed with the country's military leadership the procedures for Ukrainian units during the ceasefire.
"Information about the mirror nature of our actions and the possible extension of the ceasefire beyond Easter has also been communicated to the Russian side," he said.
– Bernama-Anadolu
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran says Lebanon stability to shape US talks in Islamabad
Article:
ISTANBUL: Iran's Foreign Ministry spokesman Esmail Baghaei said stability in Lebanon and across the region will influence the outcome of talks with the US, as consultations with Pakistani officials continue, Anadolu Ajansi reported, citing his remarks broadcast live on IRIB state TV.
Speaking from Islamabad, Baghaei said the Iranian delegation, led by Parliament Speaker Bagher Qalibaf, began consultations with Pakistani officials immediately upon arrival in the capital early Saturday.
He said Iran's positions and demands were conveyed clearly and without ambiguity, based on its proposed framework.
"Diplomacy today is a continuation of defence," he said, adding that Iran has entered what he called a "demanding diplomatic struggle" after nearly 40 days of fighting.
Baghaei said Iran remains in close coordination with its armed forces to monitor the ceasefire and respond to any potential violations.
"We have emphasised from the beginning that when we speak of a ceasefire, it must be a real ceasefire," he said.
He said that a limited number of violations occurred in the initial hours after the ceasefire was announced, adding that Iranian forces responded "swiftly and decisively".
"Since then, we have not received reports of serious violations," he said, adding that even suspected cases are addressed immediately through warnings.
Baghaei stressed that the ceasefire applies to all fronts, including Lebanon, describing it as an essential component of the broader framework.
He said Iran remains in contact with its diplomatic mission in Beirut and relevant parties to ensure commitments are upheld, underlining that developments in Lebanon will directly affect the trajectory of diplomatic efforts.
The proposed 10-point framework, as outlined by Iranian officials, includes guarantees of non-aggression against Iran, continued Iranian control over the Strait of Hormuz, recognition of Iran's right to uranium enrichment, lifting all US primary and secondary sanctions, termination of UN Security Council resolutions against Iran, termination of resolutions by the International Atomic Energy Agency Board of Governors, payment of compensation to Iran, withdrawal of US combat forces from the region, and ending hostilities across all fronts, including in Lebanon.
The high-stakes talks, dubbed the "Islamabad Talks", began soon after separate meetings between the Iranian and US delegations and Pakistan's Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif earlier in the day.
The US and Iranian delegations arrived separately in Islamabad for the talks, marking a significant high-level bilateral engagement between the two sides since 1979.
– Bernama-Anadolu
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: European Commission to tighten ChatGPT regulation - Report
Article:
BERLIN: The European Commission plans to tighten regulation of United States (US) tech company OpenAI's chatbot ChatGPT in the near future, according to the German newspaper
Handelsblatt
, reported Sputnik/RIA Novosti.
OpenAI is expected to fall under the European Union (EU)'s Digital Services Act (DSA) and thus be subject to stricter regulation, the publication noted.
According to the newspaper, the intention is to classify the service's developer, OpenAI, as a "very large online search engine" under the DSA.
This status entails additional requirements, including combating illegal content, ensuring algorithmic transparency, and providing data to researchers.
Specifically, companies are required to assess the risks of disseminating illegal information and regularly report to regulators.
Under the DSA, very large platforms and search engines are defined as services with more than 45 million monthly active users in the EU.
According to OpenAI's own data, ChatGPT's search function already significantly exceeds this threshold.
Violations of the DSA requirements could result in fines of up to six per cent of a company's global annual revenue.
– Bernama-Sputnik/RIA Novosti
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fuel supply 'stable', ministry says as Shell flags demand spikes
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry said the country's fuel supply remains "stable and sufficient".
Deputy Minister Datuk Dr Fuziah Salleh said the government, through the ministry and industry players, is closely monitoring temporary fuel supply disruptions at some petrol stations amid increased demand in several areas.
She said supply recovery measures are being carried out immediately.
"I would like to emphasise that the country's fuel supply remains stable and sufficient. The public is urged not to panic and to continue purchasing as usual.
"Cooperation from all parties is essential to ensure the situation remains under control. InsyaAllah, further updates will be provided from time to time," she said in a statement.
Fuziah was responding to
Shell Malaysia's statement
that it may face temporary spikes in petrol demand amid global oil supply volatility, which could lead to short-term fuel shortages at some of its stations.
On Thursday, Shell said it is giving priority to efforts to ensure fuel supply continuity across its retail network.
"We are closely monitoring what is a very dynamic situation and are prioritising efforts to support fuel supply continuity within our retail network.
"While we are working hard to maintain consistent supply at our stations, there may be temporary peaks in demand, resulting in shortfalls at some locations," it said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Free rabies vaccination campaign underway across Kuching this month, says SSPCA
Article:
KUCHING, April 11 — The Sarawak Society for Prevention of Cruelty to Animals (SSPCA) will be conducting its Free Rabies Vaccination Campaign across Kuching this month to help protect both pets and the wider public.
SSPCA in a Facebook post said oral rabies vaccines (ORV) have also been deployed for stray dogs as part of ongoing field efforts to curb rabies in the community.
“Over the past 2 days, our team has been on the ground in the Padawan area, undergoing training and deployment to safely administer Oral Rabies Vaccines (ORV) to stray dogs.
“This allows us to reach dogs that are harder to catch, strengthening overall rabies control in the community,” the society said.
SSPCA also stressed that ORV is not for public handling or administration to pets.
“It must be deployed by trained personnel, as each vaccine is tracked and recorded through a dedicated system once consumed by free-roaming animals.
“For owned pets, it is still best to bring them to a vaccination point for a standard rabies injection to ensure proper protection,” it said.
SSPCA will hold weekend dog vaccination sessions on Saturday and Sunday from 9am to 4pm at Batu Kawa Riverbank Park and SJK Chung Hua Syn-San-Tu.
The following weekend, sessions will be held at Balai Raya RPR Batu Kawa and Balai Raya Desa Wira, also from 9am to 4pm.
In addition, weekday oral vaccination street operations will begin next Monday until Wednesday from 7am to 10am, and continue from 4pm to 6pm in the Batu Kawa and Desa Wira areas.
The same schedule will be repeated the following week in the same locations.
SSPCA has urged the public not to feed stray dogs during morning operations on selected dates, adding that feeding may resume as usual after 10am.
Meanwhile, the Padawan Municipal Council held an ORV briefing and training programme on Friday to strengthen rabies control efforts in the state.
About 5,000 ORV baits have been procured by the government for the programme and are currently stored at the Sarawak Infectious Disease Centre under strict cold-chain conditions. — The Borneo Post

Title: Consul general urges enhanced Sarawak–Indonesia coordination to address border development challenges
Article:
KUCHING, April 11 — Stronger and more coordinated efforts between Sarawak and Indonesia are needed to advance the development of border regions, said Indonesian Consul General in Kuching Dr Abdullah Zulkifli.
Presenting a paper on strategic collaboration at an international seminar in Sambas on April 8, he said discussions among participants highlighted the need for enhanced coordination to address shared challenges, particularly in food security.
“Border cooperation can be implemented to tackle major issues such as food security.
“With better coordination, both sides can optimise resources and strengthen resilience,” he said in a statement.
He highlighted existing collaboration in the energy sector as a key example of effective cross-border cooperation.
“Indonesia and Sarawak have established energy cooperation that contributed to energy security.
“This demonstrates how such collaboration can work and why it should be expanded to other strategic sectors,” he said.
Abdullah noted that border regions such as Sambas play a strategic role as agricultural hubs with surplus production that could support food security efforts.
He also pointed out that the land border on the island of Borneo stretches about 1,881km, with more than 800km shared between West Kalimantan and Sarawak, making coordinated planning essential.
The seminar also discussed the role of key border crossings — Entikong-Tebedu, Aruk-Biawak, Badau-Lubok Antu and Jagoi Babang-Serikin — in facilitating movement and trade.
Trade between Indonesia and Sarawak has surpassed US$1 billion (RM3.96 billlion), driven by commodities such as agricultural produce, fisheries and mineral resources, he said, adding that tourism cooperation is also growing, with notable international arrivals via land crossings into West Kalimantan.
On investment, Abdullah highlighted major projects involving Sarawak Energy Berhad, including its participation in a hydropower project in Kalimantan and industrial ventures such as a bauxite smelter in Sanggau.
He said Sarawak currently exports about 190 to 200 megawatts of electricity to West Kalimantan through existing grid interconnections.
At the same time, participants acknowledged ongoing challenges such as illegal cross-border activities, regulatory differences and infrastructure gaps, which require closer bilateral coordination.
The seminar, held on April 7 and 8 in Sambas, brought together regional stakeholders and served as a platform to strengthen ties and chart future collaboration.
It was also attended by Sarawak Minister for Food Industry, Commodity and Regional Development Datuk Seri Dr Stephen Rundi Utom. — The Borneo Post

Title: MACC tracks RM203m foreign fund flows linked to ex-CEO in share sale scandal
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 11 — Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) investigators have allegedly found indications of an alleged abuse-of-power scheme involving a former chief executive officer (CEO) of a government statutory body over share sales involving public funds.
A MACC source said the individual is believed to have determined the terms and purchase price of the shares, while also acting as both proposer and approver in closed-door negotiations with minority shareholders, giving him full control over the entire process.
“Preliminary investigations also found elements of bribery and money laundering through the transfer of transaction proceeds to offshore entities to conceal the identities of recipients, including the use of nominee accounts and beneficial owners,” he said.
“Investigations also found that part of the funds was believed to have been reinvested into publicly listed companies on Bursa Malaysia, estimated at around RM30 million, to disguise the financial flows as legitimate investments,” he added.
Overall, foreign fund flows detected in the investigation are estimated to exceed US$51.3 million (RM203.4 million) and are believed to involve companies incorporated in the British Virgin Islands (BVI).
Of the total amount, six transactions involving six bank accounts in Singapore were worth more than US$48 million.
Another transaction worth about US$3.3 million was traced to Labuan and involved two beneficial owners of companies that received payments from public funds.
Two bank accounts in the United Arab Emirates (UAE), believed to belong to the former CEO, are also being investigated, involving about US$10,000 and and 37,000 UAE dirhams — together equivalent to around RM80,000.
A total of RM16.8 million has been been frozen, including six individual accounts containing around RM11 million.
MACC Senior Director of the Special Operations Division Datuk Mohamad Zamri Zainul, when contacted, confirmed that investigations are ongoing.
He said the MACC is widening the probe, including seeking cooperation from authorities in Singapore, the British Virgin Islands, the United Arab Emirates and Labuan to examine cross-border transactions and trace related assets.
